person.name: Konrad Rzentarzewski
person.bio: I was there when the config burned. In the age before observability, chaos reigned. The servers howled, and nothing scaled but entropy. Then kube cattle were born — simple, nameless beasts born of children’s games. But they multiplied. And their hunger grew vast. Years passed. The sun revolved the Earth. Then the Earth revolved the Cloud. I grew tired of debugging in darkness. I longed for light. And logs. Preferably structured. That’s when I began preaching — not loudly, just… declaratively. One apply at a time. I walked among DevOps tribes, where bash begat shell, and shell begat pain. To them I brought ConfigMaps, and Secrets hidden in plain sight. Then came the mysterious YAML — a language too holy to lint — inscribed deep within the API’s own heart. Through toil and merge, the infrastructure became manifest. Pods floated on the waters. Deployments begat replicas. Helm bore Charts. Git begat Ops. What was once dev spun to prod. And every service had a name — and every name resolved. Unless external DNS was involved. And the Preachers said: \"Behold, our logs streameth clean.\" Thus began the age of the declarative. We once dwelled in darkness. Now, we scale planetary in full light.

person.name: Tijmen Van Den Brink
person.bio: As a Lead Solution Architect I design cloud native platforms that enable organizations to run critical workloads securely and reliably. Over the past five years, I played a key role in developing a private cloud platform for the dutch government—from winning the tender through design, implementation, and operations. Building on this foundation, I'm now driving our Cloud Native portfolio, including Kubernetes as a Service and S3 Object Storage (AIstor/MinIO). My current focus is scaling these offerings across our client base. Together with my international team, we're strengthening clients' security posture through expanded services: policy enforcement with Kyverno, container runtime protection, CVE scanning, and ArgoCD for GitOps delivery. With an MSc in System and Network Engineering from the University of Amsterdam, I specialize in platform engineering and transforming successful client-specific solutions into scalable product offerings. My approach combines hands-on technical expertise with portfolio-level thinking—ensuring individual solutions benefit the wider organization.Technical focus areas: Platform as a Product (Kubernetes as a Service, Object Storage), Container security and policy enforcement (Kyverno), GitOps and CI/CD (ArgoCD, Flux) Observability (Grafana, Prometheus). What drives me is bridging the gap between client-specific innovation and reusable platform services—taking what works at scale for one organization and evolving it into offerings that deliver value across multiple contexts.

person.name: Cathy Zhang
person.bio:
Cathy is a Principal Engineer at Confluent. She has over 20 years of software design
and development experience in a wide range of areas such as serverless, large scale distributed
VM/container orchestration and scheduling, edge computing, container runtime, service mesh,
software defined network, and high availability etc.
Cathy has been an active contributor in the cloud-native computing ecosystems including
Kubernetes, OpenStack, OPNFV, and has led the development of several open-source projects
under CNCF, Linux Foundation Networking, Linux Foundation Edge and Open Infrastructure.
She has made strong contributions to the technical direction and design, driving working group
discussion, and resolving technical disagreements. She co-authored the CNCF serverless
whitepaper, the CloudEvents specification, and the OpenStack service function chaining design.
She has served as the program committee member/track chair for several Kubecons and
OpenStack summits. She has delivered talks at various open-source conferences (Kubecon,
OpenStack Summit, Open Source Summit, OPNFV).

person.name: Kenta Tada
person.bio:
Kenta Tada has worked as a Linux engineer and team lead for various organizations, specializing in Linux kernel tracing technologies and eBPF. He has contributed to the Linux kernel and the BPF Compiler Collection (BCC) to enhance kernel tracing infrastructure. He has also contributed to several CNCF-related projects, including serving as a reviewer for the system validator used in kubeadm preflight checks.

In 2023, he reviewed the Japanese translation of Learning eBPF, published by O'Reilly Japan. In 2024, he became an organizer of Cloud Native Community Japan (CNCJ), fostering open source culture in Japan. Additionally, he served as Co-Chair of KubeDay Japan 2024, playing a key role in shaping the event and promoting cloud-native technologies within the Japanese developer community.

Recognizing the growing importance of eBPF in cloud-native environments, Kenta founded the eBPF Japan Community for engineers across various industries to collaborate, share knowledge, and drive eBPF adoption in Japan.
